data.title: CEF Weekly Review: CLO Equity CEF NAVs Slide For A Second Month
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund [CEF] market activity from both the bottom-up – highlighting individual fund news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the third week of May. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company [BDC] as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

Most CEF sectors were higher on the week as Munis underperformed due to higher Treasury yields. Month-to-date, MLP and Convertible funds are in the lead due to the continued recovery in stocks.

CEF valuations continued to richen, with the median CEF sector discount now trading more than 2% tighter of its historic average level.

The credit CEF swirlogram has swerved back into the less attractive quadrant, indicating that CEF valuations are no longer particularly appealing.

Market Themes

All of the CLO Equity funds have now reported April NAVs except for EARN and OXLC. Hopefully, EARN will move to a monthly NAV reporting schedule like all the other funds. We still need to wait till the end of the month for the company to report its March NAV.

OXLC typically reports monthly numbers unless the month falls on the end of the earnings quarter, in which case the fund waits to report the NAV until it delivers the earnings, which is typically two months after the end of the quarter. Hopefully, they change this policy as OXLC will in effect go for 4 months without reporting the NAV - not a very shareholder friendly act as it keeps investors guessing as to the fund’s true valuation.

For the funds that have reported, the average NAV drop was 6.7% in April vs a drop of 5.7% for March.

The average valuation in the sector is a 7% premium with EIC being the cheapest fund (not including EARN and OXLC) at a 3% premium. ECC is most expensive at a 17% premium. While the dip in NAVs may be tempting, the overall expensive valuation makes the sector not at all appealing.

Market Commentary

The RiverNorth Capital and Income Fund (RSF) is doing a rights offering. Recall that FINS just finished its own RO. However, while FINS launched the RO because it saw an attractive time to buy assets, RSF does these programmatically as a way to make up for capital that leaves during its quarterly tender offers. At a price of $15.19 and NAV of $16.13, the subscription price would be $14.52. The discount would be tighter due to NAV dilution and is around 3.5% now post dilution.

The Convertibles-focused Ellsworth Growth and Income Fund (ECF) has boosted its distribution by 23% to $0.16 per quarter, which works out to around 6.6% on price. The catalyst was the fund’s strong performance over 2024. ECF pays a minimum of 5% on its trailing 12-month average month-end prices. Arguably, this is conservative from a longer-term return perspective as the fund’s longer-term return is north of 7%. ECF is a good example of a CEF that converts its capital gains into income even if it's not a pure equity fund.

Convertible bonds behave much like stocks due to the combination of low interest and a high equity beta. These bonds have embedded equity options which increase in value as equity prices move higher, allowing the bonds to convert into common shares after a certain strike price has been reached. At the same time, it’s easy to argue these are not income investments because they generate very little actual income. This is particularly the case for Gabelli funds that are fond of using preferreds that carry a higher coupon than the fund's assets as a source of leverage.

What this means is that the funds generate less net investment income than we see in the reports because they don’t take out preferreds dividends as these are not interest expense but a different kind of dividend. Preferreds dividends are itemized separately outside of the liabilities section. After preferreds dividends, the fund’s NII is $0.034 per quarter, which works out to 1.2% NII yield on NAV, so it’s not much for an "income" fund.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Unity Software's Trough Period Is Here - Double Digit Upside Ahead
data.description:
U Remains Attractively Valued Despite The Recent Recovery - Double Digits Upside Remains

We previously covered Unity Software Inc. (NYSE:U) in April 2025, discussing how the new management team had further turned the ship around, through the migration of its Unity advertising network to a new AI-based platform, Unity Vector, with H1'25 likely to be trough quarters.

Combined with the likely to be improved monetization, reasonable valuations, and near doubling upside potential, we had reiterated our Buy rating then.

U 1Y Stock Price

Since then, U has continued to trade sideways against the wider market's recovery by +8.7%, with the stock continuing to hold on to its recent April 2025 gains and a similar outperformance observed in its ad-tech peers in varying degrees.

Much of its recovery tailwinds are attributed to the promising advertising prospects reported by numerous market leaders, including Alphabet (GOOG), Meta (META), and Amazon (AMZN), with a similar optimism also reported by AppLovin (APP).

These developments further underscore the still robust advertising spending trends, despite J.P. Morgan Research's elevated US recession risk projections at below 50%, with it demonstrating why U has been able to report double beat FQ1'25 performance.

This is with revenues of $435M (-4.8% QoQ/ -5.5% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 19.2% (-4 points QoQ/ +2.1 YoY/ +20.1 from FY2020 levels of -0.9%), and adj EPS of $0.24 (+4.3% QoQ/ -29.4% YoY), with the QoQ/ YoY decline merely attributed to the ongoing portfolio reset well-balanced by the growing subscription revenues.

While the U management does not break out its subscription sales numbers, they already report 1.26K of customers (+0.8% QoQ/ +1.6% YoY) contributing over $100K in TTM revenues and a dollar-based net expansion rate of 97% by FQ1'25 (+1 points QoQ/ -4 YoY), up from the bottom 94% reported in FQ3'24.

These implies that the worst of the run-time fee fiasco may already be behind us, as its subscription base grows, monetization improves, and operations are optimized.

If anything, readers must note that U has completed the migration from its legacy Unity ad network to Unity Vector as of May 2025, with their self-learning models already offering "deeper insights, optimize performance, and deliver better return on investment for our customers:"

Vector is providing a 15% to 20% lift in both the number of installs and the value of in-app purchases when compared to our old model...

as the performance of Vector continues to improve, we expect to see the overall Grow business return to revenue growth, with the performance improvement from Vector outpacing any other headwinds we face. (Seeking Alpha)

For now, readers may want to temper their near-term expectations, since the management's FQ2'25 revenue guidance of $420M (-3.4% QoQ/ -6.5% YoY) and adj EBITDA of $72.5M (-13.6% QoQ/ -36.1% YoY) merely implies the minimal benefit and the additional costs linked to the late roll-out of Unity Vector, respectively.

With the additional R&D costs likely to normalize from Q3'25 onwards, aided by the transition away from its prior legacy ad models, we believe that U's bottom may already be here, with things likely to lift sequentially from Q3'25 onwards.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates remain promising, with U expected to report an excellent top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +6%/ +13.5% through FY2027, while building upon the 5Y historical top-line growth profile at a CAGR of +27.3%, with FY2024/ H1'25 being its trough years.

Most importantly, based on its positive adj EBITDA and Free Cash Flow generation despite the intensified R&D efforts, we believe that it remains well positioned to incrementally invest in its long-term prospects ahead, aided by the healthier balance sheet with lower net debts of -$0.78B (+26.4% YoY/ +36% from FY2022 levels of -$1.22B).

U Valuations

Given the recent recovery by +24.4% from the recent April 2025 bottom, it is unsurprising that U appears to be somewhat expensive at FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 28.99x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 28.16x, up from the recent bottom of 22.31x/ 26.40x and the sector median of 14.37x/ 21.98x, respectively.

On the other hand, given the annualized FQ1'25 adj EBITDA of $84M/ the management's FQ2'25 guidance of $72.5M and the consensus FY2027 estimates of $569.63M, we are looking at a relatively rich adj EBITDA growth at a 3Y CAGR of +22%.

When comparing against its ad-tech peers, including:

  • APP at FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 29.98x with a projected 3Y EBITDA growth at a CAGR of +34.8%, and
  • The Trade Desk (TTD) at 32.44x/ +17.7%, respectively,

it appears that U remains reasonably valued at current levels, attributed to the robust bottom-line growth prospects.

So, Is U Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

U 3Y Stock Price

For now, U has consistently found a floor at the established H2'24 and H1'25 bottom of $15s ranges, with it triggering a much-needed safety net for those looking to dollar cost average.

For context, we previously offered a fair value estimate of $24.60, based on the FY2024 adj EBITDA per share of $0.98 (+36.1% YoY) and the prior FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 25.15x (near to its 1Y mean of 25.23x).

This is on top of the prior long-term price target of $36.70 (currently at $36.00), based on the consensus FY2027 adj EBITDA estimates of $580.02M (currently at $569.63M) and the resultant FY2027 adj EBITDA per share estimates of $1.46 (currently at $1.43).

With their remaining double digits upside potential by +72.9% despite the recent +24.2% recovery, we believe that U continues to offer a compelling investment thesis here.

This is especially since U is likely to benefit from its completed advertising transition to Unity Vector from Q3'25 onwards, with APP already highlighting their robust ad-tech prospects in both mobile and e-commerce/ web advertising, as the latter also offers a promising FQ2'25 advertising revenue growth guidance by +77.7% YoY.

Combined with the moderating short interest volumes by -21.2% from the 2024 heights, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the U stock here.

data.title: MAG Silver: Juanicipio As One Of The Most Profitable Silver Mines Globally
data.description:
Where Is Your Heart?

After two human visitors came to my yurt over the last week, my purpose in life became much clearer. Each visitor had completely opposed views of life. After seeing them within a few hours' interval, I was able to vividly see and feel the differences between both perspectives. I could describe their respective points of view in detail, but suffice it to say that one was driven by his heart versus the other, who had a black hole instead of a glowing heart.

One was created from the inside and giving, while the other had nothing inside and was looking to fill his inner void by stealing. Both conversations lasted a few hours, so I had plenty of time to see how they think and operate. Needless to say, one won't be welcomed back, while the other wants to introduce me to his wife. See the difference? Both live on the same planet, but one is poor in all senses of the word. So which path do I want to pursue? It is quite easy to answer!

If I distil what I have learned from this experience, it is that the only thing that matters in life is where your heart is. Everything results from this life-altering decision. Can you truly and deeply love, or are you always plotting ways to extract things from others?

In a world increasingly dominated by artificial intelligence, this very light inside the human heart is under attack. Collectively, the greatest risk we face may well be the possibility of becoming soulless and losing our hearts. In today's world, loving is the greatest act of rebellion possible. This is the case precisely because it is the most challenging thing to do. After all, true wealth comes from the heart in the first place. How many financial advisors discuss the ultimate source of wealth with their clients? Do they truly care about their lives, or just want as many commissions and fees as possible?

"Do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy, and where thieves break in and steal. But store up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where moth and rust do not destroy, and where thieves do not break in and steal. For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also." Matthew 6:19

MAG Silver's Portfolio Overview

Now that I am done telling the highlight of my week in the woods, it is time to move on to my next favourite topic of the moment: silver and silver miners. I published my first research on the subject last week, discussing silver's supply and demand fundamentals in an article focused on the iShares Silver Trust (SLV) and the Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV). I then looked into miners, starting with First Majestic Silver (AG), followed by Pan American Silver (PAAS). Considering the $2.1 billion transaction between Pan American and MAG Silver (NYSE:MAG) announced earlier this month, I thought it would be valuable to learn more about MAG's world-class Juanicipio Mine located in the state of Zacatecas, Mexico.

For each year in which industrial demand grows, the silver market becomes tighter, meaning that a smaller and smaller increase in investment demand could flip the balance on prices. For investment demand to trickle down in the silver market, gold must lead the way first. Silver: A Strategic Industrial And Monetary Metal In Structural Deficit

MAG's flagship and only producing asset is a 44% ownership interest in the Juanicipio silver mine. The remaining 56% interest is owned by Fresnillo (OTCPK:FNLPF), the world's largest silver miner after producing 54.3 million ounces in 2024. As of today, I know very little about Fresnillo, but that won't last for too long, as it is the next company I plan to research in the upcoming days. How can anyone pretend to know something about the silver market if they have never researched the single largest player?

According to the 2025 World Silver Survey, primary mine production amounted to ~835 million ounces in 2024, meaning that Fresnillo and KGHM Polska have a combined market share of around 12%. In this sense, I would say that silver production is highly fragmented and largely inelastic. In the event of a large increase in spot prices, no silver producer can take the lead and boost production. Pan American Silver Or Pan American Gold?

MAG also owns the Larder Project in Ontario's Abitibi Greenstone Belt, where historical production since 1901 amounts to over 200 million ounces of gold. In addition, MAG is advancing the Deer Trail Project in Utah, with the exploration thesis of finding the source of a large carbonate replacement deposit initially discovered in 1878. Although these exploration projects may deliver some long-term optionality, they are still marginal from a financial point of view when compared to MAG's 44% interest in Juanicipio. For this reason, I won't spend much more time looking into the Larder and Deer Trail projects.

Juanicipio: One Of The World's Best Silver Mines

Juanicipio is located on the Fresnillo Silver Belt, a tiny area within Mexico from which approximately 10% of all the silver ever mined throughout history comes. Some of the mines in the area have been in continuous production since the 16th century, so it is no wonder that Mexico still accounts for ~23% of the world's primary silver production. Moreover, mining activities in Mexico are highly concentrated, as the states of Sonora and Zacatecas account for almost 60% of Mexico's mining GDP on a combined basis. On its own, the state of Zacatecas, where Juanicipio is located, accounts for almost 50% of Mexico's total silver production. In short, it would be very difficult to find a better ZIP code than that for a primary silver mine, and the ultimate proof is in the incredibly low production costs.

Similar to most silver mines, Juanicipio is polymetallic in nature. As indicated below, the mine produced ~5.5 million equivalent silver ounces in the first quarter of 2025, of which 4.0 million ounces, or 73%, came directly from silver. If we include gold in the picture, we can see that precious metals account for more than 90% of Juanicipio's revenues of $175.2 million. Of note, those figures are on a 100% owned basis, meaning they must be multiplied by 44% to arrive at MAG's share. With ~70% of its revenues coming from silver, Juanicipio is very similar to the Cerro Los Gatos silver mine from a revenue contribution perspective. As a reminder, Gatos Silver owned a 70% interest in Cerro Los Gatos and was purchased by First Majestic Silver in a $970 million transaction that closed in January 2025.

If we view First Majestic's stock as a currency, we can see a debasement that is much faster than anything seen with Western central banks. While the company issued paper to purchase assets, the added production volumes and cash flows were never enough to compensate. First Majestic Silver: Printing Paper Even Faster Than The Federal Reserve

Regarding production costs, Juanicipio's cash cost per silver ounce was -$0.91 per ounce. The negative cash costs simply mean that the ~4.0 million ounces of silver produced during the first quarter covered the entire cash costs of operating the mine, leaving the gold, zinc, and lead by-products as pure cash flows.

If we look at the entire production profile and add sustaining capital expenditures, all-in-sustaining costs increase to $10.64 per equivalent silver ounce, putting the mine in the first quartile of the industry's cost curve. To put Juanicipio's performance in perspective, Cerro Los Gatos reported all-in-sustaining costs of $13.07 per equivalent silver ounce in the first quarter of 2015, which is almost 25% higher than Juanicipio's. However, on a consolidated basis, First Majestic's all-in sustaining costs increase to $19.24 per equivalent silver ounce, or ~80% higher than MAG's pure-play exposure to Juanicipio.

We could also draw a parallel between Juanicipio and the Escobal Mine in Guatemala, owned by Pan American Silver. In 2016, the last year of full production before the ongoing mining halt, Escobal produced 21 million ounces of silver at an all-in-sustaining cost of $8.06 per ounce. Almost a decade later, there is no doubt that Escobal's production costs would increase, as energy and labour costs, among many others, are much higher. Anyhow, I think it is fair to say that once at full production, Escobal could be pretty similar to Juanicipio, both in terms of volumes and production costs. With the MAG's acquisition expected to close in the second half of 2025, Juanicipio and Escobal will likely be under the same roof pretty soon.

MAG Silver's Financial Overview

In the first quarter of 2025, Juanicipio generated operating cash flows of $86.4 million. When we deduct $10.2 million in capital expenditures, we get $76.2 million in free cash flows or the equivalent of ~$305 million on an annualized basis. Now, simply annualizing the first quarter results is unlikely to yield an accurate picture due to changes in working capital and the non-linearity of capital expenditures throughout any single year.

According to the acquisition documents released by Pan American, Juanicipio is expected to generate free cash flows on a 100% basis of $200 million in 2025, of which $88 million would be attributable to MAG's 44% ownership interest. Relative to the $2.1 billion purchase price paid by Pan American, it would represent a free cash flow yield of ~4.2%.

The closest transaction comparable that comes to mind is once again the $970 million purchase of a 70% interest in Cerro Los Gatos by First Majestic Silver. As indicated in the acquisition documents, First Majestic expects free cash flows of $70 million from Cerro Los Gatos in 2025, representing a ~7.3% yield on the price paid for the asset.

What can explain such a large difference in both transactions' implied free cash flow yields? First of all, Juanicipio is a younger asset with a much longer production profile. In fact, MAG Silver has already issued mine plans up to 2034, and this is with only 10% of the property explored. This means that Juanicipio also has a greater exploration potential, knowing that the property is in the center of the world's best ZIP code for silver mining. Considering silver's volatility, Juanicipio's lower production costs also translate into greater cash flow stability.

In terms of overall quality, I view Juanicipio as one of the best silver mines in the world, but there is also no question that Pan American paid top dollar for it. Considering silver's highly compelling supply and demand fundamentals, Pan American's team may have been willing to pay a premium to ensure long-term and low-cost exposure to the metal.

As discussed in a previous article, I view Pan American quite favourably from a capital allocation point of view. Over the last decade or so, management has done a good job replacing depleted ounces and growing overall production by issuing a reasonable number of shares. I cannot say the same with First Majestic, as I view the number of shares issued as excessive relative to the quality of the assets purchased.

Conclusion

MAG Silver would have been the perfect vehicle for me to gain exposure to silver prices. The company currently has $156 million in cash and zero debt, in addition to having a highly free-cash-flowing asset in the heart of the world's best silver mining area. Producing costs are in the first quartile of the industry's cost curve, and only a small fraction of the property is explored. MAG Silver would have been the perfect vehicle, but that was prior to the Pan American's transaction announcement.

A pure-play exposure to the highest-quality assets is exactly what I am looking for. For MAG's shareholders, the combination with Pan American will certainly provide greater diversification, but it will come at the cost of lower asset quality overall. Moreover, once the transaction is completed, Pan American expects to generate less than 30% of its revenues from silver; hence why it would be more accurate for the company to be named Pan American Gold.

With my 'Buy' rating, I want to underscore Juanicipio's tier-1 quality, along with its significant expiration potential, long mine life, and some of the lowest production costs in the industry. In a way, I would be happy if the deal fails, as it would provide the perfect opportunity to buy an initial position.

Nonetheless, even with a pure-play exposure to one of the highest-quality assets in the silver mining industry, MAG significantly underperformed spot prices over the last five years. I wonder how many mining companies have been able to sustainably outperform their metal benchmark over the long term. Considering the significant geopolitical and operational risks involved with mining, matching gold's or silver's price performance would still leave common stockholders with much greater risks and volatility. Mining is a very tough place to be invested in, indeed. So that's it for MAG Silver, I will now move to Fresnillo.

data.title: Analog Devices: Don't Jump On Conclusions From Recent Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Analog Devices (NASDAQ:ADI) just reported its Q2 2025 results and the market reacted negatively on the news, in spite of what appeared to be a strong quarter.

In spite of the stock falling by nearly 5% on the day of the release, things are changing for ADI and investors should take notice. So far, on a year-to-date basis, ADI is outperforming the broader semiconductors sector, which has not occurred in a very long time.

Therefore, it is crucial to dissect the recent quarterly results and put the recent share price into perspective in order to understand what lies ahead for ADI shareholders.

A Good Or A Bad Quarter?

On the surface, Q2 results were better-than-expected with both revenue and non-GAAP earnings per share above the consensus estimates of Wall Street analysts.

Similarly to the previous quarter, Q2 2025 revenue came above the ADI's previously provided guidance and continues on its upward trajectory since hitting bottom in the same quarter a year ago.

The upward trend is also expected to be sustained in the next quarter as management guidance for Q3 2025 was quite optimistic. Revenue for the quarter is expected to be around $2.75bn, which represents a year-on-year growth of 19%. Additionally, GAAP margin and earnings per share are also expected to improve over the coming quarter.

Outlook for the Third Quarter of Fiscal Year 2025

For the third quarter of fiscal 2025, we are forecasting revenue of $2.75 billion, +/- $100 million. At the midpoint of this revenue outlook, we expect reported operating margin of approximately 27.2%, +/-150 bps, and adjusted operating margin of approximately 41.5%, +/-100 bps. We are planning for reported EPS to be $1.23, +/-$0.10, and adjusted EPS to be $1.92, +/-$0.10.

Source: ADI Q2 2025 Earnings Release

This expected growth is meaningfully different from the current forward revenue growth of a negative 2%.

The main reason why this departure from current expectations did not result in a major positive reaction for the share price is because the market was anticipating the recent drop in revenue to be only temporary. I talked about this in early 2025 when I justified ADI's record high multiples.

Moreover, the current revenue growth rate seems to be largely due to pull-in activity from customers caused by the recent developments around tariffs.

And given some volatility sawing the quarter, we do think our auto results were aided by pull-in activity.

Source: ADI Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript

As we could see from the graph below, the Automotive segment was the major driver of revenue growth during the quarter, albeit sales in Industrial and Communications segments also increased.

Based on all that, it is quite clear why the market reacted negatively to the Q2 2025 results and why optimism around ADI's stock might not return anytime soon.

Nonetheless, it is a fact that the stock is becoming more attractively priced as its margins are gradually improving, while valuation multiples remain flat to declining on a quarterly basis.

That is why it is crucial for long-term investors to cost average and evaluate ADI's relative pricing with its improving profitability in mind.

Current Multiple In Perspective

A major point that should be made here is that the operating profitability improvements we see above were also driven by higher gross margins in recent quarters. During the last reported quarter, ADI's gross margin reached 61%, which would continue to be a tailwind for profitability on a rolling twelve month basis.

The reason why I focus on gross profitability is that the market still cares a great deal about gross margins when it comes to pricing semiconductor stocks. In spite of differences in business models, end-customers and revenue growth rates, the cross-sectional relationship between gross margins and forward price/sales multiples is quite high.

As we could see on the graph above, one of ADI's major peers in the analog semiconductors space - Texas Instruments (TXN), is priced at a similar sales multiple due to its similar gross margin. Trends in quarterly gross margins, however, are quite different when we compare ADI to TXN.

On top of that, ADI management now expects gross margin tailwinds in the coming quarters as growth of the low margin Automotive segment normalizes and especially if the high margin Industrial segment continues to grow.

(...) on the gross margin side, at the 2.75 guide, we expect to be around 70%, which we've talked about. That is -- that baked into that assumption is that we will see the growth in industrial we're anticipating, which as you guys know, is our most profitable. And that has been one of our challenges. We've talked about in getting additional leverage. If you look at just the most recent, the outperform in auto puts pressure on gross margin. But as we see the industrial leading the charts coming out with the 10% expected growth in Industrial in Q3, we do expect we will get back to around that 70% margin.

Source: ADI Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript

Based on all that, ADI seems poised to experience a multiple repricing in the coming year, provided that macroeconomic factors do not overshadow the ongoing recovery of sales.

Conclusion

Although some investors might be confused by the seemingly good second quarter and the negative price reaction following the earnings release, the share price response makes sense when taking a closer look at ADI's relative pricing and some nuances around revenue growth. Having said that, however, the stock is now priced in-line with its current business fundamentals, and as margins improve through the rest of FY 2025 I would expect ADI to continue to outperform the broader semiconductors space. Therefore, in spite of the current risks for the economy, I am changing my rating to buy.

data.title: These REITs Could Beat Realty Income
data.description:
Just recently, we shared our review of Realty Income Corporation (O), discussing the good, the bad, and the ugly.

The good news is that the "Monthly Dividend Company" enjoyed strong growth in 2024 and was able to bump up 7-Eleven to its top tenant.

The bad news is that its growth will slow down significantly in 2025.

Finally, the ugly is the rising bad debt, which raised concerns over the deteriorating portfolio quality of the REIT.

In the end, we concluded that Realty Income was potentially a good opportunity for income-oriented investors, given that it trades today at a historically low valuation and offers a high dividend yield of 5.7%. We think that its monthly dividend is secure and likely to keep growing for a long time to come, albeit at a slower pace than usual.

However, we also noted that it likely isn't one of the best REITs for 2025 if you are looking to maximize total returns. We think that there are better options in its peer group, and that's going to be the focus of today's follow-up article.

Here are two REITs that we expect to beat Realty Income over the long run. The second one may surprise you, so make sure to read till the end:

NNN REIT, Inc. (NNN)

I start the list with NNN REIT because it is the most similar to O Stock.

It is similar in that:

  • It has a multi-decade track record of dividend growth. In fact, NNN has an even longer track record than O.
  • It also has a multi-decade track record of significant market outperformance, beating the rest of the REIT sector (VNQ) and the S&P500 (SPY).
  • Finally, they both own mostly service-oriented higher-quality net lease properties with fixed rent hikes, and they combine this defensive business model with a strong investment-grade rated balance sheet.

Track record of dividend growth:

Track record of market outperformance:

But here's why NNN REIT is better than Realty Income:

Firstly, it has a better balance sheet. Their leverage ratios are roughly the same, but NNN has much longer average debt maturities at 12 years on average, compared to just 6 for Realty Income. This means that it will be less impacted by the surge in interest rates.

Secondly, NNN's portfolio is less heavily exposed to pharmacies and dollar stores, which have been some of the weakest segments of the net lease property market. Walgreens (WBA) is really struggling, closing store after store, and Dollar General (DG) and Dollar Tree (DLTR) could be some of the worst-impacted retail categories by the tariffs. Unfortunately for Realty Income, it has a lot of exposure to these properties:

Finally, NNN REIT is large enough to diversify risks, but not too big to hurt its growth. We fear that Realty Income is becoming too big for its own good and that its massive size will slow it down going forward.

Over the past few years, it was able to make up for its massive size by closing major M&A deals, buying out other REITs, but it has now run out of buyout targets and the market has punished it with a low multiple (high cost of capital), preventing it from closing similar big transactions.

NNN's size, on the other hand, is still at the sweet spot with a market cap of $8 billion. It enjoys significant economies of scale and good diversification, but new acquisitions still move the needle for them, allowing them to keep growing at a steady pace.

Meanwhile, O is now forced to step out of its circle of competence and invest in riskier properties abroad, degrading its average portfolio quality.

For these reasons, we would argue that NNN deserves to trade at a premium relative to Realty Income.

It has a better balance sheet. Better assets. Faster long-term growth prospects. And a longer track record of dividend growth.

Yet, they currently trade at the same valuation multiple, and therefore, we think that NNN is likely to deliver higher total returns going forward:

NNN REIT Realty Income
FFO Multiple 12.2x 13x
Dividend Yield 5.5% 5.7%

It seems that a lot of income investors are favoring Realty Income because it is a monthly dividend stock, but keep in mind that there is a reason why most public companies pay quarterly instead of monthly. It results in lower administrative costs, which you ultimately pay for as the shareholder. Less frequent payouts reduce costs and result in more money in your pockets.

W. P. Carey Inc. (WPC)

W. P. Carey is also one of my top REITs for 2025.

It is quite a bit different than Realty Income, but in a good way.

First, let me address the elephant in the room. Yet, W. P. Carey recently cut its dividend, and it has alienated a lot of REIT investors.

But you need to remember that the dividend cut was the result of the company's decision to spin-off its weakest assets (office net lease buildings) into a separate REIT in order to deleverage and accelerate its organic growth.

It used this as an opportunity to lower its payout ratio to retain more of its cash flow for organic growth in today's high cost of capital environment.

It initially resulted in a crash in its share price, but it has recovered since then as the market has finally recognized that this was a case of short-term pain for long-term gain:

Now, ignoring this topic, the REIT has a bright future ahead of it.

It owns mostly industrial net lease properties, which are growing in demand due to the growing trend of onshoring. Moreover, they are not as heavily exposed as retail net lease properties to the risks presented by tariffs and trade wars.

However, what makes WPC uniquely attractive relative to Realty Income is that its leases are a lot stronger. Unlike Realty Income, which has mostly fixed 1-1.5% rent escalations in its leases, W. P. Carey has mostly CPI-based rent escalations, many of which are not capped.

This resulted in much faster organic rent growth in recent years when inflation was hot and will continue to provide a tailwind for faster growth going forward, especially if inflation accelerates again:

I appreciate this a lot in today's uncertain world.

Being stuck with 1% annual rent escalations is a real risk, and Realty Income investors are not as well protected as some of these other net lease REITs.

W. P. Carey also has a decent balance sheet, but it is not quite as good as that of Realty Income. It has an investment-grade rating, its leverage is reasonable, and its maturities are well-staggered.

This year, it has guided to grow its FFO per share by 3.6%, which is nearly 3x faster than Realty Income. I expect W. P. Carey to keep growing faster going forward, and yet, both REITs trade today at similar valuations:

W. P. Carey Realty Income
FFO Multiple 12.5x 13x
Dividend Yield 5.7% 5.7%

I think that W. P. Carey is still discounted due to the recent dividend cut, which has hurt its market sentiment.

But keep in mind that Agree Realty (ADC) also cut its dividend in 2011, and it was then the most rewarding net lease REIT over the coming decade:

Eventually, investors will forget about the recent cut and refocus on fundamentals. I expect them to reprice W. P. Carey at the higher multiple that it deserves, resulting in significant outperformance relative to Realty Income.

data.title: 4 Dividend Stocks I'm Buying As The 'Big Beautiful Bill' Takes Its Toll
data.description:
It is usually only a few weeks after I resolve to withhold investing and accumulate cash for a while that the market sells off and derails my cash-raising plans.

What a cruel temptress the market is!

Even crueler is the fact that the broader indices are holding up just fine while many dividend stocks, including REITs and utilities, have sold off.

Here's a vicious paradox for you: I obviously want the stocks in my portfolio to rise in price, but I also want ample buying opportunities. Can one ever be satisfied with the current state of the market?

*Sigh*

The big news this week was the House of Representatives' passage of a major budget bill, which, among other things, rolls back clean energy tax credits faster and more thoroughly than was previously assumed. This naturally led to a big selloff in renewable stocks, which I'll discuss below.

So, in short, this week's agenda includes:

  • Why the bond market and parts of the stock market aren't happy about the big, beautiful deficit spending that the House budget virtually ensures.
  • My thoughts on the rapid phase-out of clean energy tax credits and why I think the associated selloff represents a buying opportunity for certain renewables stocks.
  • Some comments on my 4-stock buy list, including my favorite renewables stock to buy right now.

Onward!

The Big Beautiful Bill Vs. The Bond Market

The names given to Congressional bills can be almost comical.

Several years ago, Democrats passed a bill called the "Inflation Reduction Act" that had virtually nothing to do with inflation. Economists broadly agree that the bill had little to no effect on inflation in itself.

Now, in another round of marketing magic, Congress just passed the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" ("OBBBA") after a squeaker of a vote, tallied at 215 to 214.

The non-partisan Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget estimates that the bill, if approved by the Senate as-is, would add $3.1 trillion to the national debt in the next 10 years, or $310 billion per year.

However, if you exclude the extension of existing tax rates, the OBBBA increases the deficit by a much smaller amount — perhaps only $500 billion to $1 trillion over a 10-year period.

Here's the CRFB's breakdown of the OBBBA. Take a look for yourself.

Of course, you'll notice that the bill really only tinkers with the discretionary side of the federal budget, despite the fact that the largest and fastest-growing area of federal spending is entitlements.

As of 2024, entitlement spending is 3.3x larger than non-defense discretionary spending, which contains all of the spending cuts in the House budget package. Outside of interest expense, entitlement spending is growing faster than any other part of the federal government.

Charlie Munger once said, "Fish where the fish are." Likewise, when it comes to reining in government spending, it helps to look where the money is.

If the US government was serious about reducing the deficit, Congress would be willing to reform entitlements in addition to scouring the budget for cuts in discretionary areas. Several years ago, Senator Rand Paul suggested a 2% across-the-board cut imposed on every department of government, which seems like a sensible option.

Unfortunately, it appears as though Congress will not be willing to touch entitlement spending until and unless something seriously breaks that makes entitlement reform the less politically damaging option than doing nothing.

The insolvency of the major entitlement trust funds in the early 2030s may be the catalyst for such an event.

For now, the bond market is signaling discomfort with the lack of progress on deficit reduction. The 10-year Treasury yield has breached the psychologically important threshold of 4.5%, while the 30-year yield has breached the similarly important 5% level.

While the S&P 500 (SPY) has not reacted much to this mini-spike in interest rates, many rate-sensitive stocks, including REITs and other dividend payers, have been negatively impacted.

This certainly explains why many dividend stocks have sold off, bringing several back into my "buy zone."

While the SPY is down ~1.9% over the last week (as of Thursday, May 22nd), the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) is down 2.7% and the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) is down 3.9%.

While the 10-year Treasury yield looks to be on the higher end of its established range from the past few years, I don't really see a catalyst for a significant decline in interest rates.

Consumer price hikes from tariffs are inevitable, which should temporarily push up the CPI. And since the OBBBA will likely increase the fiscal deficit, there won't be any relief on the supply side of the Treasury market. Quite the opposite, actually.

Plus, the labor market seems to be holding up. Initial jobless claims remain fairly low as of the May 22nd release, and Americans are likely to continue spending money as long as they have jobs.

As I explained a few weeks ago in "Dividend Investors, It's Time To Raise Cash, Here's Why," tariff rates are now low enough to allow the US to avoid recession (and all the demand destruction it would come with) but are still high enough to ensure consumer price hikes.

The tug-of-war between recession and inflation in the 10-year Treasury yield is leaning heavily toward inflation today.

That has created a buying opportunity in rate-sensitive dividend stocks for long-term investors.

Rapid Phase-Out of Green Energy Tax Credits

Speaking of buying opportunities for long-term investors, I would argue that green/renewable energy stocks have become attractively valued in the post-OBBBA selloff.

Notably, after an 11th-hour change, the OBBBA rolled back clean energy tax credits even faster than the previous version of the bill stipulated.

To quote Politico:

In a bid to back shovel-ready projects, the changes would require projects to “commence construction” within 60 days of the bill’s enactment to qualify for tax credits and be placed in service by the end of 2028.

This is basically a repeal of the IRA, fully phasing out government support for renewables by the end of Trump's second term.

Both renewable power producers (RNRG) and the solar power industry (TAN) have been pummeled in the wake of the House passage of the budget package.

One particular loser from this bill seems to be Sunrun (RUN), the largest solar panel leasing company in the US. They own the solar panels and therefore collect the residential solar tax credits while leasing the panels to homeowners.

The previous version of the OBBBA that seemed to be advancing forward axed resi solar subsidies for homeowners but kept them in place for third-party leasing companies like RUN. After the 11th-hour update, however, the version of the bill that passed eliminated subsidies for all resi solar, including leased.

RUN plunged on the news.

This is also bad news for one of my largest holdings, Hannon Armstrong Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI), which is a lender/financier of various clean energy assets.

Residential solar is HASI's largest category of assets, and Sunrun is one of its largest partners/borrowers.

Although HASI's portfolio boasts an incredibly strong track record of performance, low historical credit losses (including before the IRA was passed), and financially durable counterparties, the worse-than-expected subsidy rollbacks in the OBBBA have triggered a selloff.

In fact, all of my green energy-related holdings sold off heavily on Thursday:

So far this week, HASI has sold off about as much as NextEra Energy Inc. (NEE), a utility company whose Energy Resources segment is the largest developer of renewables in the nation.

The next worst performance has been from Brookfield Renewable (BEP, BEPC), which not only owns a very large portfolio of operational renewable energy but also has a vast development pipeline through its various subsidiaries.

The least worst performance of my green energy holdings has been from Clearway Energy Inc. (CWEN, CWEN.A), which is a pure-play power producer. Its parent company and sponsor, Clearway Energy Group (co-owned by TotalEnergies (TTE) and BlackRock (BLK)), shoulders all development risks. By the time assets are dropped down to CWEN, they are already operational with long-term contractual revenue streams in place.

Plus, about a quarter of CWEN's cash flow derives from natural gas plants that are not affected by the rollback of clean energy subsidies.

Now, I'm not saying that there will be no negative impact on renewables companies because of this subsidy repeal. It will likely slow renewables development and blunt the electrification trend.

But I am more optimistic than Mr. Market for three key reasons.

  1. The budget bill still needs to go through the Senate, which is typically more moderate and will likely alter the OBBBA to some degree. Senate Republicans may soften the repeal of tax credits or phase them out slower than the House bill.
  2. Demand for renewable power should remain resilient even without subsidies.
  3. Democrats would likely reinstate clean energy tax credits the next time they command the authority to do so.

On the second point, see this chart from NEE, using estimates from Wood Mackenzie, showing that the levelized cost of newly built wind and solar is lower (even with battery storage adders) than that of new gas or nuclear plants that would come into service in 2030.

This is primarily due to the increased cost of building and maintaining gas and nuclear plants.

On top of that, electricity demand is increasing right now, which favors renewables that can be built and deployed much faster than new gas or nuclear capacity.

The earliest we might see newly greenlit gas plants come into service is about 2030, while new nuclear plants likely wouldn't be available until 2035 or after.

For example, the most recently completed nuclear power plant in the US was Southern Company's (SO) Vogtle units 3 and 4. Construction began in 2009. Unit 3 began commercial operation in July 2023, while Unit 4 came online in May 2024.

One of the most recently completed natural gas plants, the Guernsey Power Plant in southern Ohio, began construction in 2019 and came online in June 2023.

In contrast, most renewable power projects can be completed in a year or two. This is a significant and underappreciated non-monetary competitive advantage for renewables in a world of rising electricity demand.

Now onto the third point above.

According to a September 2024 poll from the (admittedly left-leaning) Global Strategy Group, 87% of Americans approve of clean energy tax credits. Even if that number is exaggerated by 20 points or more, popular support for green energy remains strong.

Another 2024 survey from Pew Research found that, as of May 2024, clean power sources were more popular than coal, fracking, or offshore drilling.

It would've been nice to see the popularity of clean power sources pitted against relatively low-emitting natural gas instead of the more controversial forms of fossil fuels.

But the point is that wind and solar retain support among most Americans. If and when Democrats regain power in Washington, DC, I find it highly likely that they will reinstate some measure of government support for renewables.

Therefore, with all of the above considerations in mind, I find the current selloff in renewables stocks to be an attractive buying opportunity.

My Buy List Today

I most likely won't have enough incoming cash this week to invest in anything more than a few shares of each of the following stocks, so my quest to increase my cash position (see last week's article) via the Vanguard Ultra-Short Term Bond ETF (VUSB) will have to wait.

Here are the four dividend stocks I intend to buy this week:

Dividend Yield Projected Long-Term Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
EastGroup Properties (EGP) 3.4% High-Single-Digit to Low-Double-Digit
Hannon Armstrong Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI) 6.6% Mid-Single-Digit
InvenTrust Properties (IVT) 3.5% Mid-Single-Digit
Public Storage (PSA) 4.1% Mid- to High-Single-Digit

EastGroup Properties

I don't see a compelling reason for this Sunbelt infill industrial REIT to have sold off this week other than the increase in the 10-year Treasury rate.

At about a 23x AFFO multiple and a ~3.4% dividend yield, EGP is by no means cheap on an absolute basis, but it is cheap based on its own historical valuation range. By sometime next year, the post-COVID surge in commercial real estate development should be fully absorbed, at least for the industrial sector, which should benefit EGP's top-tier and well-located portfolio of industrial parks.

Hannon Armstrong Sustainable Infrastructure

Some of my thoughts on HASI are discussed above, but I'll add here that this utterly unique clean energy financier has demonstrated a remarkable ability to maintain investment spreads through any interest rate environment.

HASI's cost of newly issued debt has gone up considerably in recent years, but so have its yields on new investments.

Given HASI's recently earned credit upgrades to investment grade status, I am even less worried about how it will perform going forward in a "higher-for-longer" interest rate environment.

InvenTrust Properties

IVT is a high-quality Sunbelt grocery-anchored retail REIT with a low-leveraged balance sheet and a conservative, shareholder-aligned management team.

There are cheaper retail REITs, to be sure, but IVT earns its premium valuation, in my view. It boasts consistently above-average same-store NOI growth rates as well as a strong (read: low) cost of capital with which to accretively expand its portfolio.

With a 3.5% yield and mid-single-digit growth, IVT is by no means a high-flyer. But it makes for a reliable ballast for a dividend growth portfolio.

Public Storage

I've been waiting for this top-tier, low-leveraged, conservatively managed self-storage REIT to drop back below $300 to finish out my position. I aim to do that this week.

If and when the housing market recovers and housing turnover increases, demand for self-storage units should likewise return to normal levels. Plus, construction of storage facilities has declined in recent years, and steel & aluminum tariffs should continue to deter the incremental development projects. This diminishes the risk of oversupply in this space.

At about a 19x AFFO multiple, PSA is a bit cheaper than its long-term average of 21x. And that 4.1%-yielding dividend is as solid as a rock.

data.title: 5 Best CEFs This Month For Yields Up To 14% (May 2025)
data.description:
Summary

For income investors, closed-end funds, or CEFs, remain an attractive investment class that covers various asset classes and promises high distributions and reasonable total returns.

Closed-end funds are generally characterized by higher volatility and deeper drawdowns than the broader market. For these reasons, they are not suited for everyone.

In this monthly series, we highlight five CEFs with solid track records that pay high distributions and offer “excess” discounts. We try to separate the wheat from the chaff using our filtering process to select just five CEFs every month from around 500 closed-end funds.

Introduction:

The market has recovered all of its losses since the so-called liberation day (April 2nd) and, in fact, is now trading roughly at the same level as early January. The volatility has subsided, but there are still concerns about sustained inflation and a possible recession. During the recent market sell-off, CEFs also lost ground in sync with the broader market, but overall, good funds handled it reasonably well. Now, since the market has recovered, the opportunity for wider discounts has closed as well. So, investors have to tread carefully as prices are no longer cheap; however, uncertainties remain.

Why CEFs?

The broader market, as represented by the S&P 500, provides a dismal level of yield of less than 1.30%. Income investors and retirees can't survive on that level of income unless they have a very large pool of capital. Alternatively, one can find solid blue-chip individual dividend stocks that pay much higher than the S&P 500. They can also provide market-matching growth on a longer-term basis. To identify such stocks, we also publish a monthly article on the "5 Best DGI stocks." You can find the most recent one here.

However, with CEFs, we can take the income to the next level. That said, they generally come with higher risks (discussed later), so we believe that an allocation of 20% to 30% to a diversified CEF portfolio should be good enough for most income investors. In summary, these investments should be used primarily for income, not growth.

We publish this monthly article on CEFs (Closed-end funds) to provide our readers with a select list of 5 to 10 CEFs that, as a group, provide wide diversification in terms of underlying assets, solid long-term performance at least on a relative basis, and are trading at decent valuations. Above all, the income goal for the group is to provide a yield that is at least 8% or higher.

This month, as a group, the selected funds are offering an average distribution rate of about 11.2% (as of 05/16/2025). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had a decent record over the long term and collectively returned 7.3% and 15.2% in the last three and five years, annualized. The average discount (to NAV) is reasonable, at nearly -5.5%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Why Is It Important To Have A Diversified CEF Portfolio?

We believe a well-diversified CEF portfolio should contain at least 10 CEFs, preferably from different asset classes. It's also advisable to build the portfolio over a period rather than invest in one lump sum. If you were to invest in one CEF every month for a year, you would have a well-diversified CEF portfolio by the year's end. What we provide here every month is a list of five probable candidates for further research. A CEF portfolio can be an important component of the overall portfolio strategy. One should preferably have a DGI portfolio as the foundation, and the CEF portfolio could be used to boost the income level to the desired level. How much should one allocate to CEFs? Each investor needs to answer this question himself/herself based on their personal situation and factors like the size of the portfolio, income needs, risk appetite, or risk tolerance.

Criteria to Shortlist:

Criteria

Brings down the number of funds to...

Reason for the Criteria

Baseline expense < 2.5% and Avg. Daily Volume > 10,000

Approx. 435 Funds

We do not want funds that charge excessive fees. Also, we want funds that have fair liquidity.

Market capitalization> 100 Million

Approx. 400 Funds

We do not want funds that are too small.

Track record/ History longer than five years (inception date 2016 or earlier)

Approx. 375 Funds

We want funds that have a reasonably long track record.

Discount/Premium < +7%

Approx. 350 Funds

We do not want to pay too high a premium; we want bigger discounts.

Distribution (dividend) Rate > 5%

Approx. 250-290 Funds

The current distribution (income) is reasonably high.

5-Year Annualized Return on NAV > 0% AND/ OR

3-Year Annualized Return on NAV >0%

Approx. 200-250 Funds

We want funds with a reasonably good past track record in maintaining their NAVs.

After we applied the above criteria this month, we are generally left with around 250 funds (plus or minus 5%) on our list. From here on, we apply our other criteria to narrow the list.

Note: All tables in this article have been created by the author (unless explicitly specified). Most of the data in this article are sourced from Cefconnect.com, Cefa.com, and Morningstar.com.

Narrowing Down To 50 Funds

To narrow down the number of funds to a more manageable count, we will shortlist ten funds based on each of the following criteria. Thereafter, we will apply certain qualitative criteria to each fund and rank them to select the top five.

At this stage, we also eliminate certain funds with substantially negative NAV returns for both three-year and five-year periods.

Seven Broad Criteria:

  • Excess discount/premium (explained below).
  • Distribution rate.
  • Return on NAV, last three years (medium-term).
  • Return on NAV, last five years (long term).
  • Coverage ratio.
  • Excess return over distributions.
  • The total weight (calculated up to this point).

Excess Discount/Premium:

We certainly like funds that are offering large discounts (not premiums) to their NAVs. But what's important is to look at the "excess discount/premium" and not the absolute value. We want to see the discount (or premium) relative to their past record, say 52-week average.

Excess Discount/Premium = Current Discount/Premium (Minus) 52-Wk Avg. Discount/ Premium.

For more explanation of the excess discount and z-scores, please refer to our blog post (link provided at the beginning of the article).

We sort our list (of approx. 250 funds) on the "excess discount/premium" in descending order. For this criterion, the lower the value, the better it is. So, we select the top 10 funds (most negative values) from this sorted list.

(All data as of 05/16/2025)

Table 1:

High Current Distribution Rate:

After all, most investors invest in CEF funds for their juicy distributions. Even though we should not chase yield, at this stage, we want to give them a fair representation in our list and subject them to a more rigorous criterion later on. We sorted our list on the current distribution rate (descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds from this sorted list.

Table 2:

Medium-Term Return on NAV (last three years):

We then sort our list on a three-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and select the top 10 funds. Please note that the huge drawdowns in the case of MLPs during the pandemic (caused by the Oil crash) and subsequent recovery have led to too many MLPs and energy funds topping this list (3-year NAV gains). For this reason, we make a shortlist based on the total score and keep only a few of them on this list.

Table 3:

Five-Year Annualized Return on NAV:

We then sorted our list on the five-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 4:

Coverage Ratio (Distributions Vs. Earnings):

The coverage ratio is derived by dividing the earnings per share by the distribution amount for a specific period. Please note that in some cases, the coverage ratio may be a bit inaccurate since the "earnings per share" maybe three to six months old. But in most cases, it's fairly accurate. Furthermore, the coverage ratio is not critical for pure equity funds as they generate very little dividend income, and they cover most of their distributions from capital gains. However, we sorted our list on the coverage ratio and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 5:

Excess Return Over Distribution:

If a fund provides a very high distribution but low returns over 3, 5, or 10-year periods, it does not help the long-term interests of the investor. In simple terms, the fund is just overpaying the distributions and hurting the NAV in the long term. That is why we calculate a factor we like to call "excess return." This excess return is what is provided by the fund over and above the distribution rate over a specified time period. We calculate it by subtracting the distribution rate from the average of three-year and five-year NAV returns. In this list, we included the top 10 funds.

Table 6:

Total Weight (Quality Score) Calculated Up to This Point:

Note: The Total Weight calculation is not fully completed at this point since we have not considered the 10-year NAV return. However, we select the top 15 names based on current calculations.

Table 7:

From the selections so far, we have 75 funds in total (tables 1-7).

We will need to see if there are any duplicates among them. In our current list of 75 funds, there were 28 duplicates, meaning there are funds that appeared more than once. The following names appear twice (or more):

Appeared twice: ADX, BANX, EFR, EIC, ETY, GGN, KIO, KYN, NML, PEO, and XFLT (11 duplicates).

Appeared three times: CET, EMO, MXF, and SPE (8 duplicates).

Appeared four times: IIF, PDX, and TWN (9 duplicates).

Once we remove 28 duplicate rows, we are left with 47 (75- 28) funds.

Note: It may be worthwhile to mention here that just because a fund has appeared multiple times does not necessarily make it an attractive candidate. Sometimes, a fund may appear multiple times simply for the wrong reasons, like a high current discount, high excess discount, or a very high distribution rate that may not be sustainable. However, during the second filtering stage, it may not score well on the overall quality score due to other factors like a poor track record. That said, if a fund has appeared four times or more, it may be worth a second look.

Narrowing Down to Just 10 Funds

In our list of funds, we may already have some of the best probable candidates. However, so far, they have been selected based on one (or two) criteria that each may be good at. That's not nearly enough. So, we will apply a combination of criteria by applying weights to eight factors to calculate the total quality score and filter out the best ones.

We will apply weights to each of the eight criteria:

Criteria

Max. Weight

Comment

Baseline expense

If less than ten years of history, an average of three and five-year returns is taken.

Excess NAV return over distribution rate
